figures.gloss: The supreme, unmanifest creator of the universe. Existing as a void of absolute potential, he initiated creation by speaking twenty-two sacred words, vibrating the cosmos into existence before withdrawing to let lesser spirits shape the earth.
figures.facet: He is not a ruler to be prayed to, but an impersonal architect of frequency and vibration. He embodies the cosmic truth that speech and sound are the literal building blocks of matter.
figures.provenance: Composite entry: the 'twenty-two sacred words' cosmology derives from Amadou Hampâté Bâ's Komo/Kore-tradition informants (via Youssouf Tata Cissé); the Bemba/Ngala creator-and-withdrawal frame is Germaine Dieterlen's 'Essai sur la religion Bambara' (1951) and Dominique Zahan's later work. The entry synthesizes both source-streams; this is not a single unified indigenous doctrine attested whole in either source alone.

figures.gloss: The androgynous water spirit and restorer of cosmic equilibrium. Following the chaos of his twin, Pemba, Faro reorganized the world, establishing the Niger River, the seasons, and the moral order of human society.
figures.facet: Faro represents pure dynamic balance rather than mere fertility. He constantly purifies the universe by absorbing and neutralizing the dangerous, volatile energies released by human transgression and bloodshed.
figures.provenance: Germaine Dieterlen 'Essai sur la religion Bambara' (1951).

figures.gloss: The ambitious earth spirit and twin of Faro. Descending as a spinning balanza tree, he sought to dominate creation, generating the first human beings but introducing discord, theft, and the corruption of the original cosmic plan.
figures.facet: Pemba is not a pure devil but a tragic demiurge. He represents the unchecked principle of expansion, earthly rootedness, and the selfish ego that breaks cosmic harmony in pursuit of power.
figures.provenance: Germaine Dieterlen 'Essai sur la religion Bambara' (1951); Patrick McNaughton.

figures.gloss: A mythical half-human, half-roan antelope who taught the Bamana the sacred art of agriculture. He scratched the soil with his claws to introduce farming, intimately linking human survival to the rhythms of the earth and sun.
figures.facet: He physically embodies the punishing, noble communion of labor, sun, and soil. When humanity grew lazy, he buried himself in the earth, leaving only his wooden headdress as a strict reminder of hard work.

figures.gloss: The universal, invisible energy of action, life, and consequence. It animates the cosmos and resides in all things, but becomes dangerously concentrated through spoken words, spilled blood, iron-smelting, and the breaking of oaths.
figures.facet: It is strictly amoral. Like electricity, it empowers or pollutes depending entirely on the ritual armor, caste, and esoteric knowledge of the one who attempts to wield it.

figures.gloss: The most powerful and secretive of the Bamana initiation societies (jow), exclusively governed by blacksmiths. It polices the community and combats sorcery using terrifying masks encrusted with dense sacrificial matter.
figures.facet: Komo does not rely on prayer or spirit intercession; it operates through the aggressive, instrumental mastery of nyama. Its mask is not a representation of a god, but a living battery of concentrated power.

figures.gloss: A dense, amorphous or zoomorphic power object built around a secret core, such as bone or bark. It is heavily crusted with sacrificial blood, chewed kola nut, and earth to create a massive reservoir of nyama.
figures.facet: The boli intentionally blurs the line between object and organism. It is a man-made god, engineered specifically to trap raw spiritual energy and deploy it for its owner's defensive or aggressive ends.
figures.provenance: Sarah Brett-Smith 'The Making of Bamana Sculpture' (1994).

figures.gloss: The endogamous blacksmith caste of the Bamana. They are the carvers of masks, operators of the Komo society, and masters of fire, uniquely equipped by bloodline to handle the extreme nyama released by smelting and circumcision.
figures.facet: The smith stands outside normal social hierarchy. He wields an occult, transformative power that protects the village precisely because his mastery over fire and iron renders him socially transgressive and deeply feared.

figures.gloss: The 'little old woman with the white head,' Pemba's female counterpart born of his restless wandering across the earth. Betrayed and abandoned by Pemba, her grief-frenzy scattered impurity, toil, and suffering into the world, and is said to have introduced the practice of ritual excision.
figures.facet: Disorder given a face and a motive: where Pemba's chaos is ambition, Muso Koroni's is heartbreak — the tradition roots agricultural toil and initiatory cutting alike in a betrayed woman's rage rather than in cosmic malice.
figures.provenance: Germaine Dieterlen, 'Essai sur la religion Bambara' (1951); Dominique Zahan's studies of Bamana cosmology develop the Pemba/Muso Koroni complex further.

figures.gloss: The first of the six jow, the initiation society every uncircumcised boy entered before any other — organized into five graded years under the horned Ntomo mask, teaching silence, endurance, and discipline of mouth and body.
figures.facet: Personhood built like a foundation course: before a Bamana boy can enter any higher society, Ntomo first drills the raw capacities — silence, patience, bodily control — on which every later initiation depends.
figures.provenance: Germaine Dieterlen and Dominique Zahan's ethnography of the jow ladder; Zahan's monographs on Bamana masking describe the Ntomo mask's horn-and-comb iconography.

figures.gloss: The sixth and terminal jo, in which initiates ritually die and are reborn through ascending grades — including the tradition's sacred clown-figures — toward the fullest human approach to divine knowledge available in Bamana religion.
figures.facet: The ladder's answer to its own first rung: where Ntomo disciplines the child's body, Kore dissolves and remakes the adult's whole understanding of the divine — initiation's beginning and end bracketing an entire religious education.
figures.provenance: Dominique Zahan, 'Sociétés d'initiation bambara: le n'domo, le koré' (1960) — the core monograph on this society.

figures.gloss: The sequenced system of six initiation societies — Ntomo, Komo, Nama, Kono, Chiwara, and Kore — through which Bamana religious knowledge and full personhood are built stage by stage across a lifetime, each society restricted to those who have passed the one before.
figures.facet: Religion here is not a doctrine held all at once but a curriculum: divinity is disclosed only in installments, gated by age-grade and ordeal, so what one knows depends entirely on how far one has been initiated.
figures.provenance: Germaine Dieterlen and Dominique Zahan's joint ethnographic mapping of the jow system; summarized comparatively by Patrick McNaughton.

figures.gloss: The tutelary spirit of a village's founding pact, seated in a designated tree, grove, or stone at the settlement's edge, and honored with periodic sacrifice to keep the community's land-bond and safety intact.
figures.facet: Sovereignty over territory made literal and local: unlike the trans-village powers of the jow, the dasiri belongs to one place and one founding agreement — the village's own private contract with the land it stands on.
figures.provenance: Documented in French colonial-era and later ethnography of Bamana and neighboring Mande village religion (cf. Germaine Dieterlen's field reports on village cult practice).

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The silent, distant creator and the active, androgynous organizer who realizes the creator's spoken will on earth.
Intra-tradition: The perfect, cooling equilibrium of water directly opposes the arrogant, chaotic ambition of the dry earth.
Intra-tradition: The endogamous blacksmith caste and the terrifying secret society they exclusively operate.
Intra-tradition: The secret society relies directly on the dense, blood-encrusted altars as their primary engines of spiritual power.
Intra-tradition: The specialist caste whose inherited bloodline allows them to survive the dangerous manipulation of universal energy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme, remote creator who shapes the initial universe through signs and vibration before withdrawing.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The unmanifest, infinite creator who speaks reality into existence through alphabetic or linguistic vibration.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Deep structural parallel in West African cosmologies regarding the androgynous, water-based twin who restores cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cooling, balancing, and predominantly feminine/androgynous principle of creation that neutralizes volatile energy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ambitious, disruptive twin who descends prematurely to dominate the earth, permanently introducing chaos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The arrogant demiurge responsible for the flawed material world, acting against the supreme harmony of the cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The localized earth/animal figure whose intimate connection with the soil and eventual burial yields the secret of agriculture.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The divine civilizer who introduces agriculture to humanity before physically retreating into the earth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The invisible, potent, and strictly amoral energy of the universe that must be safely managed by specialized individuals.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The dynamic, impersonal cosmic energy that underlies all existence and demands heavy ritual management to prevent disaster.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Man-made power objects intentionally packed with organic, sacrificial, and symbolic matter to house and direct raw spiritual force.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The master of iron, fire, and occult energy who possesses the unique, highly dangerous capacity to shape human society.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The liminal, supernaturally powerful smith figure whose mastery over metal renders him dangerous and distinct from normal society.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An esoteric societal structure that transforms individuals and polices the community through profound secrecy, terror, and the revelation of cosmic law.
Pemba's betrayed consort, whose grief unleashed impurity and toil into a world he had already begun to corrupt.

Faro's restored cosmic equilibrium directly answers the disorder Muso Koroni's frenzy released — the tradition's two feminine or androgynous water/earth powers stand at opposite poles of the same crisis.
Ntomo is the entry rung of the six-stage ladder the jow names as a whole.
Kore is the terminal rung completing the ladder the jow names as a whole.
The first and last societies of the initiatory ladder — discipline of the child bracketing rebirth of the adult.
Komo, already in the roster as the senior blacksmith-governed society, is one named stage within the jow ladder this concept describes as a whole.
Cross-tradition: both are place-bound tutelary spirits charged with a defined community's protection and founding pact, requiring regular sacrifice to remain bound to their people.
Cross-tradition: both root a community's sanctity in a specific grove or tree, tended and never violated, as the physical seat of its protective power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: closed West African initiatory secret societies with their own ranks, secret languages, and jurisdiction reaching into communal law.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: power objects built up and fed over time to house and concentrate spiritual force at a fixed site.
Cross-tradition: both are closed male initiatory societies whose masked or voiced spirit-presences are in fact managed secrets known only to the initiated, kept from women and the uninitiated.

dossier.essence: The traditional religion of the Bamana (Bambara), the Mande-speaking agriculturalists who form Mali's largest ethnic group, historically centered on the middle Niger around Segou. It combines a withdrawn creator (Maa Ngala / Ngala / Bemba), a cosmogony of rival twin powers (Faro and Pemba), an impersonal vital energy (nyama) released by speech, blood, and craft, and a ladder of initiation societies (jow) that dispense esoteric knowledge by degrees. Most Bamana today are Muslim; the jow, boliw altars, and masquerades persist unevenly alongside and beneath Islam.
dossier.cosmos: In the cosmogony recorded by Dieterlen, creation issues from primordial vibration and speech — the creator speaks the world's elements into being — after which the ambitious earth-twin Pemba descends and corrupts the order, and the androgynous water-twin Faro re-balances it, laying down the Niger, the seasons, and social law. All things carry nyama, an amoral energy concentrated by killing, iron-smelting, potent words, and oath-breaking; the cosmos is less governed than managed. The dead join the ancestors and remain parties to clan life, their power likewise a nyama matter requiring rite.
dossier.practice: Men pass through graded initiation societies — classically six: N'domo, Komo, Nama, Kono, Chi Wara, and Kore — each holding masks, altars, and knowledge disclosed only to initiates; the senior Komo, run exclusively by blacksmiths, polices sorcery and enforces communal order. Ritual technology centers on boliw, dense sacrificial power-objects fed with blood and organic matter, and on masquerades such as the Chi Wara antelope dances honoring farming labor. The endogamous specialist castes (nyamakala) — blacksmiths (numuw), bards, leatherworkers — handle the most dangerous concentrations of nyama.
dossier.canon: An oral and initiatic corpus — cosmogonic recitation, society liturgies, and esoteric sign-systems — with no scriptures; the principal written syntheses are 20th-century ethnographies (Dieterlen 1951; Zahan 1960; McNaughton 1988).
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds religion as power-management — the sacred as an amoral energy handled through graded secrecy, caste specialization, and engineered objects rather than worshiped as a person.
sources: Germaine Dieterlen, 'Essai sur la religion bambara' (1951)
Dominique Zahan, 'Societes d'initiation bambara: le N'domo, le Kore' (1960)
Patrick R. McNaughton, 'The Mande Blacksmiths: Knowledge, Power, and Art in West Africa' (1988)
Sarah C. Brett-Smith, 'The Making of Bamana Sculpture: Creativity and Gender' (1994)
